title: File System Operations
description: Write files from reactive state and persist app state to disk, sliced from the uptime-monitor example.
write_effect_intro: The uptime-monitor's status page component turns reactive state into a file on disk. A <0>createEffect</0> re-renders the HTML whenever a probe result changes, and <1>catchError</1> keeps a bad render from crashing the app:
write_effect_notice: The effect tracks <0>props.overall()</0> and <1>rows()</1> synchronously, then hands the finished HTML to an async publish step. Reading signals before the first await is what keeps the dependency graph accurate.
publish_intro: The actual disk write is a plain async function built on <0>node:fs/promises</0>. It creates the output directory on demand and overwrites <1>./out/status.html</1> in place:
publish_notice: Nothing here is CReact-specific — the reactive system decides when to write, and ordinary Node.js APIs do the writing. Every effect run produces a fresh, complete file.
file_memory_intro: The other file system pattern every app needs is persistence. <0>FileMemory</0> implements the <1>Memory</1> contract with one JSON file per stack:
file_memory_notice: <0>getState</0> returns null when no ledger exists yet, and <1>saveState</1> rewrites the full deployment state after every change. This is the ledger the example apps use to survive restarts.
see_walkthrough: See the full app in the <0>uptime-monitor walkthrough</0>.
